# Preface {.unnumbered}

This guide grew out of frustration with a pattern I kept seeing in my own lab. Talented early career researchers would produce manuscript drafts that were theoretically exhaustive but argumentatively weak. Every relevant theory got a paragraph. Every related concept got a mention. The papers were comprehensive and unreadable.

The problem was not knowledge. My students knew the literature well, sometimes too well. The problem was a set of unwritten rules nobody had told them about: how to decide which theories are load-bearing for your argument and which are not, how to write for a reader who needs to be convinced rather than impressed, and how to survive a review process that sometimes rewards the wrong things.

I work in cognitive psychology, at the intersection of perception, memory, and narrative cognition. It is a field rich in theory and, at times, poor in argument. We have more frameworks than we have crucial tests between them. I have watched gifted researchers spend months writing introductions that cover the landscape rather than staking a position in it, and I have read the reviews that follow: specific, detailed, and asking for three more theories.

This problem has gotten worse. The arrival of AI writing tools has accelerated a tendency that was already there. Ask an AI assistant to improve your introduction and it will suggest theories you have not mentioned. The writing comes back polished and bloated, and it sounds authoritative enough that the underlying problem, a missing argument, becomes harder to see.

This guide is my attempt to approach the unwritten rules. It is not a comprehensive textbook on scientific writing; there are excellent books for that. It is a practical field guide for researchers who already know their science and are struggling with turning it into a paper that works. The final chapter addresses AI writing tools directly, not to discourage their use, but to help you use them as a sharp tool rather than a crutch.

The specific problem this guide addresses, too many theories and too little argument, is most acute in theory-rich fields like psychology, where phenomena attract multiple competing accounts and reviewing culture reinforces citation of all of them. In fields with tighter theoretical consensus, or where data more reliably rule out competing accounts, the pressure is lower. But psychology is not one of those fields, and neither are most of the disciplines adjacent to it.

The concept of strong inference comes from a 1964 paper by the physicist John Platt [-@plattStrongInference1964].
 Platt observed that some fields were advancing far faster than others, and traced the difference to a specific habit of mind: devising competing hypotheses and designing experiments that could exclude at least one of them. He was writing for bench scientists. But his core insight applies directly to the problem this guide addresses. A theoretical framing that cannot say which result would count against which theory is not doing scientific work. The filters in this guide are, among other things, a way of asking Platt's question at the level of the manuscript: does this theory belong here because it can lose, or because it feels relevant?

The examples throughout are drawn from event cognition research, which is where most of my own work lives. But the principles apply wherever you write a paper that makes a theoretical claim. If you work in an adjacent field, the norms around theoretical framing may differ from what is described here; treat the specific advice as a starting point rather than a prescription.

If you are a PhD student preparing your first manuscript, a postdoc revising a rejected paper, or a supervisor looking for something to hand your students, this is for you.
